title: Cannot bind host port to macvlan IP address
body:
<!--
---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST

**NOTE** A large number of issues reported against Podman are often found to already be fixed
in more current versions of the project.  Before reporting an issue, please verify the
version you are running with `podman version` and compare it to the latest release
documented on the top of Podman's [README.md](../README.md).  If they differ, please
update your version of Podman to the latest possible and retry your command before creating
an issue.

Also, there is a running list of known issues in the [Podman Troubleshooting Guide](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/master/troubleshooting.md),
please reference that page before opening a new issue.

If you are filing a bug against `podman build`, please instead file a bug
against Buildah (https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues). Podman build
executes Buildah to perform container builds, and as such the Buildah
maintainers are best equipped to handle these bugs.
-->

**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**

I'm trying to run two containers on the same host, both publishing the same port. I have defined the following network:
```json
{
  "cniVersion": "0.4.0",
  "name": "macvlan",
  "plugins": [
    {
      "type": "macvlan",
      "mode": "bridge",
      "master": "eth0",
      "ipam": {
        "type": "host-local",
        "ranges": [
          [
            {
              "subnet": "192.168.1.0/24",
              "rangeStart": "192.168.1.226",
              "rangeEnd": "192.168.1.255",
              "gateway": "192.168.1.1"
            }
          ]
        ],
        "routes": [
          {
            "dst": "0.0.0.0/0"
          }
        ]
      }
    }
  ]
}
```
I can run two containers, assigning each one different IP address within the range. But I can't publish the same port on both containers. 

If I use `-p 80:80` then the second container cannot start due to a port conflict (I guess that's because it's trying to bind on 0.0.0.0 on both containers).

If I use `--ip 192.168.1.226 -p 192.168.1.226:80:80` then I get the following error:

> cannot listen on the TCP port: listen tcp4 192.168.1.226:80: bind: cannot assign requested address

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. Add network using config shown above

2. `podman run -it --network macvlan --ip 192.168.1.226 -p 192.168.1.226:80:80 busybox`

**Describe the results you received:**

> Error: cannot listen on the TCP port: listen tcp4 192.168.1.226:80: bind: cannot assign requested address

**Describe the results you expected:**

Container starts successfully with port 80 published to the host on the macvlan IP.

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
Version:      2.1.1
API Version:  2.0.0
Go Version:   go1.15.2
Built:        Thu Jan  1 01:00:00 1970
OS/Arch:      linux/arm
```

**Output of `podman info --debug`:**

```

  arch: arm

  conmon:

  cpus: 4

  os: linux

    path: /run/podman/podman.sock
  rootless: false
  slirp4netns:

  swapFree: 104853504

  imageStore:

  runRoot: /var/run/containers/storage

```

**Have you tested with the latest version of Podman and have you checked the Podman Troubleshooting Guide?**

Yes

**Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):**

Physical (Raspberry Pi 4, running Raspbian)

comments.body:
@baude I don't think we expect this to work, right (specifically the combination of `-p` with a macvlan network).

@fenech The idea of a macvlan network is that you will have access to a real interface on the host - so you would not need to use `-p` because ports opened on the macvlan interface are already publically accessible?
@mheon yeah, that makes sense, I think that this boils down to a lack of understanding on my part. If that's the case then this issue can be closed, thanks for your time.
Should we make the options conflict so that this would be clearer to users?
We'd have to read the CNI network in at container creation time, to make sure it didn't have the portmap plugin enabled - could be expensive?
